title: Safe medication disposal and blister pack recycling
subtitle: Returning medicines to the pharmacy, disposing of blister packs via Terracycle, and why household bin disposal causes water contamination
excerpt: Unused and expired medications flushed down the sink or placed in the household bin contaminate water courses and landfill. Most pharmaceuticals pass through standard water treatment and accumulate in rivers and groundwater. The correct disposal route is the pharmacy take-back scheme, which is free and available at all UK pharmacies. Blister packs (the aluminium and plastic push-through packaging) cannot be returned to pharmacies but can be recycled via Terracycle drop-off schemes.
sourceNotes: NHS England guidance on medication disposal. UK Environment Agency research on pharmaceuticals in the water environment. European Medicines Agency guidance on environmental risk of medicines. Terracycle UK blister pack recycling scheme guidance. gov.uk National Health Service (Charges and Pharmaceutical Services) Regulations on pharmacy take-back obligations.

glossaryTerms.definition: The presence of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) in water bodies as a result of excretion, inappropriate disposal, or manufacturing waste. Endocrine disruptors from oral contraceptives and painkillers have been detected in UK rivers at concentrations that affect fish reproduction.

Blister packs are made from aluminium foil and PVC or PVDC plastic laminated together. The materials are recyclable separately but not together. Standard kerbside recycling cannot separate them. Terracycle operates drop-off schemes at some pharmacies and GP surgeries that separate and recycle both materials.

content.text: Up to 45 percent of UK prescriptions are returned unused to pharmacies each year, representing around 300 million pounds of wasted medicine.
has been found at harmful levels in UK rivers that receive outflow from water treatment works. Returning medicines to a pharmacy prevents most of this.
Prescription medicines: all pharmacies must take back unused prescription medicines at no charge.
Over-the-counter medicines: most pharmacies accept these as well, though they are not legally required to.
Liquid medicines and injections: accepted; keep in original container.
Sharps (needles, lancets): go in a sharps bin, available free on prescription from GP. The full sharps bin is collected by the local council.
Blister packs are not accepted by pharmacy take-back schemes (only the medicines themselves are accepted).

via Terracycle is the only widely available UK route. Search the Terracycle website for the nearest drop-off point: many Boots pharmacies and some GP surgeries participate. The packs do not need to be cleaned but should be empty. Blister packs that go in the household bin landfill, because the laminated aluminium-PVC composite is not kerbside-recyclable.
Why not flush medicines down the drain

UK water treatment plants cannot remove most drug compounds. A study in 2019 found 33 drug ingredients in English rivers above safety limits. Common ones included ibuprofen, diclofenac, the contraceptive pill's hormones, and antidepressants.

attrs.symptom: No Terracycle drop-off for blister packs nearby

content.text: The nearest drop-off may be a Boots pharmacy 10 to 15 miles away. Accumulate blister packs in a sealed bag over several months and make one trip when the bag is full. Alternatively, contact your local GP surgery to suggest they join the Terracycle blister pack programme.
